metadata.title: Honourable Minister of Power & Steel
metadata.source_volume: Policies of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, Volume Three, Section Seven
metadata.implementing_committee: Electric Power Sector Reform Implementation Committee (EPIC)
metadata.oversight_body: National Council on Privatisation (NCP) under Vice-President Atiku Abubakar

context.sector_condition: Completely failed to attract investment. Generation, transmission and distribution all degraded. Electricity demand could not be met from existing consumers.
context.government_diagnosis: NEPA technically and commercially broken. Government cannot fund sector development alone. Private sector participation essential.
context.reform_driver: Privatisation as cardinal economic programme of the Obasanjo administration

short_to_medium_term_3_to_5_years: Efficient, safe, affordable and cost-effective electricity throughout Nigeria
Attract private investment from Nigeria and overseas
Develop transparent and effective regulatory framework — NERC
Government divestment from state-owned electricity entities
Full liberalisation of the electricity market
Prepayment meters to be installed expeditiously
Billing and collection progressively concessioned to private sector
Tariffs to cover full operating costs and profit on new investments by 2002-3
long_term_beyond_5_years: Universal access to electricity — explicitly stated as NOT necessarily through the grid
Aggressive targets for rural electrification programme
Domestic production of electrical equipment in Nigeria
Nigerian electricity companies to become global leaders
Flexible regulatory environment responsive to new technologies
institutional_promises: Nigerian Electricity Regulatory Commission (NERC) to be established as independent regulator
NEPA to be unbundled into generation, transmission and distribution companies
Licenses to be issued to private operators in the Electricity Supply Industry (ESI)
Annual reports on electricity development to be published
National Electricity Forum to be organised by Ministry of Power and Steel
Implementation machinery to start immediately after policy approval

off_grid_acknowledgement.exact_provision: Universal access to electricity, although not necessarily through the grid
off_grid_acknowledgement.chapter: Chapter Two, Section 2.2 Long-Term Objectives, item c
off_grid_acknowledgement.significance: FIRST official Nigerian government acknowledgement that off-grid solutions are a legitimate pathway to universal electricity access
off_grid_acknowledgement.vitics_implication: The government knew off-grid was a solution in 2001. No programme followed until 2015. This 14-year gap is a core Vitics intelligence signal explaining why the private solar market filled the vacuum.

tariff_timeline.commitment: Cost-reflective tariffs to be achieved by time of privatisation — i.e. by 2002-3
tariff_timeline.what_happened: Tariffs remained politically manipulated and subsidised for 20+ years. Partial cost-reflective tariffs only partially introduced under Band A classification in 2023 — 20 years late
tariff_timeline.consumer_impact: Consumers paid confusing, inconsistent, politically-set bills for two decades

privatisation_timeline.original_target: Privatisation to begin immediately, complete within 3-5 years — by 2004-2006
privatisation_timeline.what_happened: Privatisation actually completed in 2013 — 7-10 years late
privatisation_timeline.ipp_capacity_caps: Maximum guaranteed IPP capacity: 800MW in 2001, 700MW in 2002

prepayment_meters.commitment: Install prepayment meters expeditiously — NEPA instructed to proceed immediately
prepayment_meters.what_happened: Prepaid meter rollout took 15+ years and remained incomplete well into the 2010s

consumer_reality.assessment: Policy created legal and institutional scaffolding — NERC, privatisation framework, tariff principles — but delivered zero consumer-visible outcomes in the period immediately following publication
consumer_experience_2001_2013: NEPA continued as primary grid operator despite reform intentions
PHCN (Power Holding Company of Nigeria) replaced NEPA in name only — same problems, new acronym
Generator dependency deepened significantly — Nigeria became world's largest per-capita generator market
Estimated billing remained the norm — consumers had no metering transparency
Consumer vocabulary: NEPA wahala, PHCN = Please Have Candles Nearby
No rural electrification programme launched under NEPP
consumer_reality.generator_market_growth: Generator imports and sales increased substantially 2001-2013 as consumers lost faith in grid improvement
consumer_reality.household_energy_cost_burden: MSMEs spending 25-40% of operating costs on diesel for generators by mid-2000s

policy_to_reality_gap.gap_scale: 1-10 where 10 = complete policy failure at consumer level
policy_to_reality_gap.summary: Almost total disconnection between policy ambition and consumer experience. Every major consumer-facing commitment was either delayed by a decade or never implemented.

specific_gaps.reality: Achieved partially only in 2023 — 20 years late
No off-grid programme for 14 years after acknowledgement
No specific funded programme launched under NEPP

institutions_created.name: Nigerian Electricity Regulatory Commission (NERC)
institutions_created.role: Independent electricity sector regulator — licensing, tariffs, standards
institutions_created.consumer_relevance: Became visible to consumers primarily through tariff decisions and DISCO regulation

institutions_created.name: Electric Power Sector Reform Implementation Committee (EPIC)

sdg7_affordable_clean_energy.direction: Intended positive — actual impact near zero for consumers 2001-2013

sdg10_reduced_inequalities.relevance: Medium — rural electrification mentioned but not programmed
sdg10_reduced_inequalities.direction: No meaningful impact on energy inequality

sdg13_climate_action.relevance: Low — environmental impact mentioned once in long-term objectives
sdg13_climate_action.direction: No emissions or clean energy provisions

transition_note: NEPP set legal foundations but was not a consumer transition marker. The real transition began with the 2013 privatisation completion when consumers finally lost faith in grid promises and began actively seeking alternatives.

vitics_analysis_instructions.when_analysing_2001_2013_content: Content from this period reflects deep consumer frustration with grid failure and growing generator dependency. NEPP promises were invisible to consumers. Any positive energy sentiment in this period is likely about generator technology or early solar adopters, not grid improvement.
vitics_analysis_instructions.key_signal_to_watch: References to NEPA/PHCN failure, generator costs, darkness. These are the dominant energy consumer signals of the NEPP era.
vitics_analysis_instructions.off_grid_signal: Any pre-2013 discussion of solar or off-grid options represents early adopter discourse — wealthy households, NGO-funded projects, telecoms towers. Not mass market.
vitics_analysis_instructions.policy_gap_signal: If consumers in this period mention government electricity promises, the expected sentiment is cynicism and disappointment — the NEPP era trained Nigerian consumers to distrust government energy announcements.

document_quality.scan_quality: TapScanner — readable but some pages have bleed-through from reverse side
document_quality.reading_method: Visual rasterization at 150 DPI — pages 1, 2, 3, 9, 10, 11, 21, 28, 33, 45 read
document_quality.confidence: High — key chapters fully read including preface, objectives, tariff policy, implementation
document_quality.pages_not_read: Chapters 7-15 covering detailed regulatory, environmental and specific sector provisions — not critical for consumer intelligence purposes
